additionalTime: 2 to 8 hours steak marinade, 10 minutes chile soak, 10 minutes steak rest, plus 10 minutes potato steam-dry time
author: Robert Carr
description: Carne asada burritos with citrus-garlic marinated steak, savory Mexican-style rice, creamy pinto beans, extra-crispy seasoned potatoes, Oaxaca-Jack cheese, avocado crema, quick pickled onions, a toasted flour tortilla, and a smooth hot-oil-simmered roasted arbol-tomatillo drip hot sauce on the side.

groceryIngredients.item: flap steak, skirt steak, or flank steak
Oaxaca cheese or low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella

ingredients: 1 pound flap steak, skirt steak, or flank steak
1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the steak, or 3/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
3 tablespoons fresh orange juice, from about 1 orange
2 tablespoons fresh lime juice for the marinade
1 1/2 tablespoons avocado oil for the marinade and steak, divided
1 1/2 teaspoons Worcestershire sauce
1/2 teaspoon low-sodium soy sauce
1 1/2 teaspoons minced chipotle pepper in adobo
3 cloves garlic for the marinade, finely grated or minced
1/2 teaspoon cumin for the marinade
1/2 teaspoon Mexican oregano
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the marinade
1/2 teaspoon brown sugar
2 teaspoons fresh lime juice for finishing the steak
1 small pinch kosher salt for finishing the steak, only if needed
1/2 cup long-grain white rice
2 teaspoons avocado oil for the rice
1 tablespoon finely minced white onion for the rice
1 clove garlic for the rice, finely minced
2 teaspoons tomato paste for the rice
3/4 cup plus 2 tablespoons chicken broth for the rice
1 bay leaf, optional
1/4 teaspoon cumin for the rice
1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the rice, or 1/8 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1 tablespoon finely chopped cilantro for the rice
2 teaspoons fresh lime juice for the rice
1 15-ounce can pinto beans, drained but not rinsed
1/4 cup chicken broth or water for the beans
2 teaspoons avocado oil for the beans
1/4 small white onion for the beans, finely diced
1 clove garlic for the beans, minced
1/2 teaspoon cumin for the beans
1/4 teaspoon black pepper for the beans
1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the beans, or 1/8 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
10 ounces Yukon Gold potatoes, cut into 1/2-inch cubes
2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt for parboiling the potatoes
1/8 teaspoon baking soda for parboiling the potatoes
1 1/2 tablespoons avocado oil for crisping the potatoes, divided
1/4 teaspoon smoked paprika for the potatoes
1/4 teaspoon garlic powder for the potatoes
1/4 teaspoon cumin for the potatoes
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the potatoes, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/8 teaspoon black pepper for the potatoes
1/2 avocado
3 tablespoons sour cream
1 tablespoon fresh lime juice for the avocado crema
2 tablespoons cilantro for the avocado crema
1/2 small garlic clove for the avocado crema
1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the avocado crema
1/2 small red onion, thinly sliced
3 tablespoons apple cider vinegar for the pickled onions
3 tablespoons water for the pickled onions
1 1/2 teaspoons granulated sugar
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the pickled onions
2 large 12-inch burrito-size flour tortillas
2 ounces Oaxaca cheese, freshly shredded, or low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella
2 ounces Monterey Jack cheese, freshly shredded
2 tomatillos, husked and rinsed
1 Roma tomato
2 jalapenos
1/4 small white onion for the hot sauce
3 unpeeled garlic cloves for the hot sauce
4 dried chile de arbol peppers, stems removed
1/4 cup hot water for soaking the toasted chiles
1/2 teaspoon tomato paste for the hot sauce
2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar for the hot sauce
1 tablespoon fresh lime juice for the hot sauce
1 to 3 tablespoons water for thinning the hot sauce
1 1/2 tablespoons avocado oil for the hot sauce
1/2 teaspoon honey
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the hot sauce, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the hot sauce
instructions: Trim any large pieces of silver skin from the 1 pound flap steak, skirt steak, or flank steak, then pat the steak very dry with paper towels.
Season both sides of the steak with 1 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 3/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt.

In a bowl or zip-top bag, mix 3 tablespoons fresh orange juice, 2 tablespoons fresh lime juice, 1 tablespoon avocado oil, 1 1/2 teaspoons Worcestershire sauce, 1/2 teaspoon low-sodium soy sauce, 1 1/2 teaspoons minced chipotle pepper in adobo, 3 grated or minced garlic cloves, 1/2 teaspoon cumin, 1/2 teaspoon Mexican oregano, 1/2 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/2 teaspoon brown sugar.
Add the salted steak to the marinade, turn until coated, cover, and refrigerate for at least 2 hours or up to 8 hours.

Make the quick pickled onions by combining 1/2 thinly sliced small red onion, 3 tablespoons apple cider vinegar, 3 tablespoons water, 1 1/2 teaspoons granulated sugar, and 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt in a bowl.
Stir the pickled onions well, press them down into the liquid, and let them sit while the rest of the recipe is prepared.

Make the roasted arbol-tomatillo drip hot sauce by placing 2 tomatillos, 1 Roma tomato, 2 jalapenos, 1/4 small white onion, and 3 unpeeled garlic cloves in a dry cast-iron skillet over medium-high heat.

Cook the hot sauce vegetables for 8 to 12 minutes, turning occasionally, until the tomatillos soften, the garlic skins are browned in spots, and the tomatoes, jalapenos, and onion are charred in places.
Remove the garlic cloves as soon as they are browned and softened, let them cool briefly, peel them, and transfer the peeled roasted garlic to a blender.
Transfer the roasted tomatillos, tomatoes, jalapenos, and onion to the blender.
Toast 4 dried chile de arbol peppers in the dry skillet for 15 to 30 seconds, just until fragrant and slightly darkened.
Transfer the toasted chiles to a small bowl, cover them with 1/4 cup hot water, and soak for 10 minutes so the hot sauce blends smoother and less papery.
Drain the soaked chiles, discard the soaking water, and transfer the chiles to the blender.

Add 1/2 teaspoon tomato paste, 2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar, 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice, 1 tablespoon water, 1/2 teaspoon honey, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper to the blender.
Blend the hot sauce until very smooth.
For a smoother drip sauce, pass the blended hot sauce through a fine-mesh strainer before simmering; for a more rustic sauce, leave it unstrained.
Heat 1 1/2 tablespoons avocado oil in a small saucepan over medium heat until shimmering.
Carefully pour the blended hot sauce into the hot oil; it may splatter at first.
Simmer the hot sauce over medium-low heat for 4 to 6 minutes, stirring often, until the raw vinegar edge rounds off, the color deepens slightly, and the oil looks integrated.
Add 1 to 2 tablespoons more water only if needed to make the hot sauce loose enough to drip from a spoon.
Taste the hot sauce and adjust with a small pinch of salt, a few drops of lime juice, or a few drops of honey only if needed.
Rinse 1/2 cup long-grain white rice under cool water until the water runs mostly clear, then drain well.
Heat 2 teaspoons avocado oil in a medium saucepan over medium heat.
Add the drained rice and toast for 2 to 3 minutes, stirring often, until the rice smells nutty and some grains turn lightly opaque.
Add 1 tablespoon finely minced white onion and 1 finely minced garlic clove, then cook for 30 seconds.
Add 2 teaspoons tomato paste and 1/4 teaspoon cumin, then stir for 30 seconds so the tomato paste darkens slightly.
Add 3/4 cup plus 2 tablespoons chicken broth, 1 bay leaf if using, and 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Bring the rice to a boil, stir once, cover, reduce the heat to low, and cook for 15 minutes.
Turn off the heat and let the rice steam, covered and undisturbed, for 10 minutes.
Remove the bay leaf, fluff the rice, then fold in 1 tablespoon finely chopped cilantro and 2 teaspoons fresh lime juice.
Make the creamy pinto beans by heating 2 teaspoons avocado oil in a small saucepan over medium heat.
Add 1/4 small finely diced white onion and cook for 3 to 4 minutes, until softened.
Add 1 minced garlic clove, 1/2 teaspoon cumin, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, then cook for 30 seconds.
Add 1 drained 15-ounce can pinto beans and 1/4 cup chicken broth or water.
Simmer the beans for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring often, until creamy but not soupy.
Mash about half of the beans against the side of the pan so the mixture becomes spreadable and can act as the burrito glue.
Make the crispy potatoes by placing 10 ounces 1/2-inch Yukon Gold potato cubes in a saucepan and covering them with cold water by 1 inch.
Add 2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt and 1/8 teaspoon baking soda to the potato water.
Bring the potatoes to a boil, then simmer for 5 to 6 minutes, until the outsides are slightly tender but the centers are not falling apart.
Drain the potatoes very well, return them to the empty warm pan, and shake the pan gently for 15 to 20 seconds to rough up the potato edges without breaking the cubes apart.
Spread the potatoes on a rimmed baking sheet and let them steam dry for 10 minutes.

Toss the dried potatoes with 1 1/2 tablespoons avocado oil, 1/4 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/4 teaspoon cumin, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper.
Heat a cast-iron skillet over medium-high heat until hot.
Cook the potatoes in a single layer with space between pieces, working in batches only if your skillet is small.
Cook for 8 to 10 minutes, turning every few minutes, until crisp and browned on several sides.

Make the avocado crema by blending or mashing 1/2 avocado, 3 tablespoons sour cream, 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice, 2 tablespoons cilantro, 1/2 small garlic clove, and 1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt until smooth.
Remove the steak from the marinade and scrape off any heavy garlic or herb clumps so they do not burn.
Pat the steak very dry with paper towels; this is important for browning instead of steaming.
Heat a cast-iron skillet or grill pan over medium-high to high heat until very hot.
Add up to 1 tablespoon avocado oil only if the pan looks dry.
Sear the steak in batches if needed so the pan stays very hot and the steak browns instead of steams.
Sear the steak for 3 to 5 minutes per side, depending on thickness, until deeply browned and the center reaches 130 to 135 degrees F for medium-rare or 140 to 145 degrees F for medium.
For USDA guidance, cook beef steaks to 145 degrees F with a 3-minute rest; for medium-rare preference, use fresh intact steak and cook to 130 to 135 degrees F if that is your desired doneness.
Transfer the steak to a cutting board and rest for 10 minutes.
Slice the rested steak thinly against the grain, then cut the slices into bite-size pieces so the burrito is easy to bite through.
Toss the sliced steak with any resting juices, 2 teaspoons fresh lime juice, and a small pinch of kosher salt only if the steak tastes like it needs it.
Mix 2 ounces freshly shredded Oaxaca cheese or low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella and 2 ounces freshly shredded Monterey Jack cheese in a bowl.
Warm each 12-inch flour tortilla in a dry skillet for 20 to 30 seconds per side, or until soft, flexible, and lightly toasted in spots.
Assemble 1 burrito at a time by placing a warm tortilla on a clean work surface.
Spread about 1/3 cup creamy pinto beans just below the center of the tortilla, leaving a wide border on the sides.
Add about 1/3 cup Mexican-style rice over the beans.
Add about 1/3 cup crispy potatoes over the rice.
Add half of the sliced carne asada over the potatoes.
Sprinkle 2 ounces Oaxaca-Jack cheese over the hot steak.
Add 1 to 2 tablespoons avocado crema and a small forkful of drained pickled onions.
Fold the left and right sides of the tortilla over the filling, then roll tightly from the bottom into a compact burrito.
Place the burrito seam-side down in a dry skillet over medium heat.
Toast the burrito for 1 to 2 minutes per side, starting with the seam side, until lightly crisp, sealed, and browned in spots.
Repeat with the remaining tortilla and fillings.
Serve each toasted burrito with the roasted arbol-tomatillo hot sauce on the side.
Drip a small spoonful of hot sauce onto each bite as you eat instead of pouring it into the burrito, which keeps the tortilla from getting soggy.
notes: Best steak choice: flap steak is the easiest high-reward option because it has strong beef flavor, good fat, and a forgiving texture. Skirt steak is excellent but can be chewier if sliced wrong. Flank steak works well but is leaner.

Do not marinate the steak overnight. The citrus is useful, but too much time in an acidic marinade can make the surface texture mushy. For skirt steak, stay closer to 2 to 4 hours; for flap or flank steak, 4 to 8 hours works well.
The marinade keeps Worcestershire as the main savory background note and uses only 1 teaspoon soy sauce so the steak tastes like carne asada instead of steakhouse beef.
The steak must be patted very dry before searing. Wet marinade left on the surface prevents browning and can make the steak taste steamed.
Sear the steak in batches if needed. Crowding the skillet traps steam and weakens the crust.
Slice the steak against the grain, then cut it into bite-size pieces. Long strips pull out of the burrito when you bite into it.
Tossing the sliced steak with its resting juices and a little lime wakes the steak back up after searing and resting.
The rice is intentionally more savory than plain cilantro-lime rice. Tomato paste, onion, garlic, broth, and cumin make it taste integrated with the burrito instead of like a separate side dish.
The beans should be creamy and spreadable, not loose. They act as a moisture barrier and help hold the burrito together.
This recipe intentionally makes extra rice and beans. Use only about 1/3 cup of each per burrito so the burritos roll tightly and do not become heavy or mushy.
The potatoes add crisp texture, absorb steak juices, and make the burrito more satisfying without turning it watery.
Baking soda and roughing up the potato edges help create a starchy exterior that browns and crisps harder.
Steam-drying the potatoes before crisping matters. Wet potatoes brown poorly and soften faster inside the burrito.
Cook the potatoes in a roomy single layer, or in 2 batches if your skillet is small. Crowded potatoes steam instead of crisp.

Freshly shredded Oaxaca and Monterey Jack melt better than pre-shredded cheese. If Oaxaca is hard to find, low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella is the best substitute because it gives a similar mild flavor and stretchy melt. Avoid fresh mozzarella here because it is too wet for a burrito.
Drain the pickled onions before adding them to the burrito. Their job is brightness and crunch, not extra liquid.
Use large 12-inch flour tortillas if possible. Smaller tortillas make overstuffed burritos that tear or cannot be rolled tightly.
Toast the assembled burrito seam-side down first. It seals the fold, improves texture, and gives the tortilla a lightly crisp exterior.
The hot sauce is intentionally served outside the burrito. Dripping it onto each bite gives heat, acid, and roasted chile flavor without soaking the tortilla.
Roasting the hot sauce garlic unpeeled lowers the risk of scorching the cloves and making the sauce bitter.
Soaking the toasted chile de arbol peppers before blending makes the hot sauce smoother, rounder, and less papery.

Simmering the blended hot sauce in avocado oil makes the roasted chile flavor rounder, softens the raw vinegar edge, and gives it a smooth texture that drips cleanly but still lightly coats each bite.
With a high-powered blender such as a Vitamix 5200, the sauce can be very smooth without straining, but straining gives the cleanest restaurant-style drip texture.
For a milder hot sauce, use 3 to 4 dried chile de arbol peppers instead of 8 and remove the jalapeno seeds before blending.
For a hotter sauce, use 10 to 12 dried chile de arbol peppers or add 1 serrano with the jalapenos.
The hot sauce should be loose enough to drip but not watery. Add water 1 tablespoon at a time only after blending.
For USDA guidance, cook beef steaks to 145 degrees F with a 3-minute rest. The medium-rare range is included for people who prefer that doneness when using fresh intact steak.
Eat these right after the final skillet toast.
servings: 2 large burritos, with extra rice and beans
title: Carne Asada Burritos with Crispy Potatoes and Roasted Arbol-Tomatillo Drip Hot Sauce
totalTime: 1 hour 55 minutes plus 2 to 8 hours steak marinade
